title: Online Hyperparameter Meta-Learning with Hypergradient Distillation

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## ONLINE HYPERPARAMETER META-LEARNING WITH HYPERGRADIENT DISTILLATION

**Hae Beom Lee**[1] **, Hayeon Lee**[1] **, Jaewoong Shin**[3] **, Eunho Yang**[1] _[,]_[2] **, Timothy M. Hospedales**[4] _[,]_[5] **, Sung Ju Hwang**[1] _[,]_[2] KAIST[1] , AITRICS[2] , Lunit[3] , South Korea, University of Edinburgh[4] , Samsung AI Centre, Cambridge[5] , United Kingdom _{_ haebeom.lee, hayeon926, shinjw148, eunhoy, sjhwang82 _}_ @kaist.ac.kr _{_ t.hospedales _}_ @ed.ac.uk

## ABSTRACT

Many gradient-based meta-learning methods assume a set of parameters that do not participate in inner-optimization, which can be considered as _hyperparameters_ . Although such hyperparameters can be optimized using the existing gradientbased hyperparameter optimization (HO) methods, they suffer from the following issues. Unrolled differentiation methods do not scale well to high-dimensional hyperparameters or horizon length , Implicit Function Theorem (IFT) based methods are restrictive for online optimization, and short horizon approximations suffer from short horizon bias. In this work, we propose a novel HO method that can overcome these limitations, by approximating the second-order term with knowledge distillation. Specifically, we parameterize a single Jacobian-vector product (JVP) for each HO step and minimize the distance from the true second-order term. Our method allows online optimization and also is scalable to the hyperparameter dimension and the horizon length. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on two different meta-learning methods and three benchmark datasets.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Meta-learning (Schmidhuber, 1987; Thrun & Pratt, 1998) aims to learn a learning process itself over a task distribution. Many gradient-based meta-learning approaches assume a set of parameters that do not participate in inner-optimization (Lee & Choi, 2018; Flennerhag et al., 2019; Raghu et al., 2019) which can be seen as _hyperparameters_ . Those hyperparameters are important in helping the inner-learner converge faster and generalize better. As they are usually very high-dimensional such as element-wise learning rates (Li et al., 2017), we cannot meta-learn them with simple hyperparameter optimization (HO) techniques such as random search (Bergstra & Bengio, 2012) or Bayesian optimization (Snoek et al., 2012) due to the too extensive search space.

In this case, we can use gradient-based HO methods that can directly optimize the highdimensional hyperparameters by minimizing the validation loss w.r.t. the hyperparameters (Bengio, 2000). Due to the expensive computational cost of evaluating the hypergradients (i.e. the gradient w.r.t. the hyperparameters), there has been a lot of efforts to improve the effectiveness and the efficiency of the algorithms.

However, unfortunately, none of the existing algorithms satisfy the following criteria at the same time that should be met for their practical use: _1) scalable to hyperparameter dimension, 2) online optimization, 3) memory-efficient, 4) avoid short-horizon bias_ . Please See Table 1 for the comparison of existing gradient-based HO algorithms in the above four criteria.

Forward-Mode Differentiation (FMD) (Franceschi et al., 2017) in Table 1 is an algorithm that forward-propagates Jacobians (i.e. derivatives of the update function) from the first to the last step, which is analogous to real-time recurrent learning (RTRL) (Williams & Zipser, 1989) in recurrent neural networks. FMD allows online optimization (i.e. update hyperparameters every inner-step)

1

with the intermediate Jacobians and also computes the hypergradients over the entire horizon. However, a critical limitation is that the time and space complexity linearly increases w.r.t. the hyperparameter dimension. Thus, we cannot use FMD for solving many practical meta-learning problems that come with millions of hyperparameters, which is the main problem we tackle in this paper.

Secondly, Reverse-Mode Differentiation (RMD) (Maclaurin et al., 2015) back-propagates the Jacobian-vector products (JVPs) from the last to the initial step, which is structurally identical to backprop through time (BPTT) (Werbos, 1990). RMD is scalable to the hyperparameter dimension, but the space complexity linearly increases w.r.t. the horizon length (i.e., the number of innergradient steps used to compute the hypergradient). It is possible to reduce the memory burden by checkpointing some of the previous weights and further interpolating between the weights to approximate the trajectory (Fu et al., 2016). However, RMD and its variants are not scalable for online optimization. This is because they do not retain the intermediate Jacobians unlike FMD and thus need to recompute the whole second-order term for every online HO step.

Thirdly, algorithms based on Implicit Function Theorem (IFT) are applicable to high-dimensional HO (Bengio, 2000; Pedregosa, 2016). Under the assumption that the main model parameters have arrived at convergence, the best-response Jacobian, i.e. how the converged model parameters change w.r.t. the hyperparameters, can be expressed by only the information available at the last step, such as the inverse of Hessian at convergence. Thus, we do not have to explicitly unroll the previous update steps. Due to the heavy cost of computing inverse-Hessian-vector product, Lorraine et al. (2020) propose to approximate it by an iterative method, which works well for high-dimensional HO problems. However, still it is not straightforward to use the method for online optimization because of the convergence assumption. That is, computing hypergradients before convergence does not guarantee the quality of the hypergradients.

To our knowledge, the short horizon approximation such as one-step lookahead (1-step in Table 1) (Luketina et al., 2016) is the only existing method that _fully_ supports online optimization, while being scalable to the hyperparameter dimension at the same time. It computes hypergradients only over a single update step and ignores the past learning trajectory, which is computationally efficient as only a single JVP is computed per each online HO step. However, this approximation suffers from the short horizon bias (Wu et al., 2018) by definition.

In this paper, we propose a novel HO algorithm that can simultaneously satisfy all the aforementioned criteria for practical HO. The key idea is to distill the entire second-order term into a single JVP. As a result, we only need to compute the single JVP for each online HO step, and at the same time the distilled JVP can consider longer horizons than short horizon approximations such as one-step lookahead or first-order method. We summarize the contribution of this paper as follows:

- We propose _HyperDistill_ , a novel HO algorithm that satisfies the aforementioned four criteria for practical HO, each of which is crucial for a HO algorithm to be applied to the current meta-learning frameworks.

- We show how to efficiently distill the hypergradient second-order term into a single JVP.

- We empirically demonstrate that our algorithm converges faster and provides better generalization performance at convergence, with three recent meta-learning models and on two benchmark image datasets.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Hyperparameter optimization** When the hyperparameter dimension is small (e.g. less than 100), random search (Bergstra & Bengio, 2012) or Bayesian optimization (Snoek et al., 2012) works well. However, when the hyperparameter is high-dimensional, gradient-based HO is often preferred since random or Bayesian search could become infeasible. One of the most well known methods for gradient-based HO are based on Implicit Function Theorem which compute or approximate the inverse Hessian only at convergence. Bengio (2000) computes the exact inverse Hessian, and Luketina et al. (2016) approximate the inverse Hessian with the identity matrix, which is identical to the onestep lookahead approximation. Pedregosa (2016) approximates the inverse Hessian with conjugate gradients (CG) method. Lorraine et al. (2020) propose Neumann approximation, which is numerically more stable than CG approximation. On the other hand, Domke (2012) proposes unrolled differentiation for solving bi-level optimization, and Shaban et al. (2019) analyzes the truncated unrolled differentiation, which is computationally more efficient. Unrolled diffrentiation can be fur-

2

ther categorized into forward (FMD) and reverse mode (RMD) (Franceschi et al., 2017). FMD is more suitable for optimizing low-dimensional hyperparamters (Im et al., 2021; Micaelli & Storkey, 2020), but RMD is more scalable to the hyperparameter dimension. Maclaurin et al. (2015) proposes a more memory-efficient RMD, which reverses the SGD trajectory with momentum. Fu et al. (2016) further reduce memory burden of RMD by approximating the learning trajectory with linear interpolation. Luketina et al. (2016) can also be understood as a short horizon approximation of RMD for online optimization. Our method also supports online optimization, but the critical difference is that our algorithm can alleviate the short horizon bias (Wu et al., 2018). RMD is basically a type of backpropagation and it is available in deep learning libraries (Grefenstette et al., 2019).

**Meta-learning** Meta-learning (Schmidhuber, 1987; Thrun & Pratt, 1998) aims to learn a model that generalizes over a distribution of tasks (Vinyals et al., 2016; Ravi & Larochelle, 2016). While there exists a variety of approaches, in this paper we focus on gradient-based meta-learning (Finn et al., 2017), especially the methods with high-dimensional hyperparameters that do not participate in inner-optimization. For instance, there have been many attempts to precondition the innergradients for faster inner-optimization, either by _warping_ the parameter space with every pair of consecutive layers interleaved with a warp layer (Lee & Choi, 2018; Flennerhag et al., 2019) or directly modulating the inner-gradients with diagonal (Li et al., 2017) or block-diagonal matrix (Park & Oliva, 2019). Perturbation function is another form of hyperparameters that help the inner-learner generalize better (Lee et al., 2019; Ryu et al., 2020; Tseng et al., 2020). It is also possible to let the whole feature extractor be hyperparameters and only adapt the last fully-connected layer (Raghu et al., 2019). On the other hand, some of the meta-learning literatures do not assume a task distribution, but tune their hyperparameters with a holdouot validation set, similarly to the conventional HO setting. In this case, the one-step lookahead method (Luketina et al., 2016) is mostly used for scalable online HO, in context of domain generalization (Li et al., 2018), handling class imbalance (Ren et al., 2018; Shu et al., 2019), gradient-based neural architecture search (Liu et al., 2018), and coefficient of norm-based regularizer (Balaji et al., 2018). Although we mainly focus on metalearning setting in this work, whose goal is to transfer knowledge through a task distribution, it is straightforward to apply our method to conventional HO problems.

## 3 BACKGROUND

In this section, we first introduce RMD and its approximations for efficient computation. We then introduce our novel algorithm that supports high-dimensional online HO over the entire horizon.

## 3.2 REVERSE-MODE DIFFERENTIATION AND ITS APPROXIMATIONS

Basically, RMD is structurally analogous to backpropagation through time (BPTT) (Werbos, 1990). In RMD, we first obtain _αT_ = _[∂][L]_[val] _∂w_[(] _[w] T[T][ ,][λ]_[)] and back-propagate _A_ and _B_ from the last to the first step in the form of JVPs (See Algorithm 1). Whereas RMD is much faster than FMD as we only need to compute one or two JVPs per each step, it usually requires to store all the previous weights _w_ 0 _, . . . , wT −_ 1 to compute the previous-step JVPs, unless we consider reversible training with momentum optimizer (Maclaurin et al., 2015). Therefore, when _w_ is high-dimensional, RMD is only applicable to short-horizon problems such as few-shot learning (e.g. _T_ = 5 in Finn et al. (2017)). **Trajectory approximation.** Instead of storing all the previous weights for computing _A_ and _B_ , we can approximate the learning trajectory by linearly interpolating between the last weight _wT_ and the initial weight _w_ 0. Algorithm 2 illustrates the procedure called DrMAD (Fu et al., 2016), where each ˆ intermediate weight _wt_ is approximated by _wt_ = �1 _− T[t]_ � _w_ 0 + _T[t][w][T]_[for] _[ t]_[ = 1] _[, . . . , T][−]_[1][.] _[A]_[ and] _B_ are also approximated by _A_[ˆ] _s_ = _[∂]_[Φ][(][ ˆ] _w∂sw_ ˆ _−s_ 1 _−,λ_ 1; _Ds_ ) and _B_[ˆ] _t_ = _[∂]_[Φ][(] _w_[ ˆ] _t−∂λ_ 1 _,λ_ ; _Dt_ ) , respectively. However, although DrMAD dramatically lower the space complexity, it does not reduce the number of JVPs per each hypergradient step. For each online HO step _t_ = 1 _, . . . , T_ we need to compute 2 _t −_ 1 JVPs, thus the number of total JVPs to complete a single trajectory accumulates up to[�] _[T] t_ =1[(2] _[t][−]_[1) =] _[ T]_[ 2][,] which is definitely not scalable as an online optimization algorithm.

**Short-horizon approximations.** One-step lookahead approximation (Luketina et al., 2016) is currently one of the most popular high-dimensional online HO method that can avoid computing the excessive number of JVPs (Li et al., 2018; Ren et al., 2018; Shu et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018; Balaji et al., 2018). The idea is very simple; for each online HO step we only care about the last previous step and ignore the rest of the learning trajectory for computational efficiency. Specifically, for each step _t_ = 1 _, . . . , T_ we compute the hypergradient by viewing _wt−_ 1 as constant, which yields _dwdλt[≈][∂w] ∂λ[t]_ �� _wt−_ 1[=] _[B][t]_[(See][Eq.][(][3][)).][Or,][we][may][completely][ignore][all][the][second-order][deriva-] tives for computational efficiency, such that _[dw] dλ[t][≈]_[0][(][Flennerhag][et][al.][,][2019][;][Ryu][et][al.][,][2020][).] While those approximations enable online HO with low cost, they are intrinsically vulnerable to short-horizon bias (Wu et al., 2018) by definition.

Each _gs_[SO] requires to compute only one or two additional JVPs. Also, as we use DrMAD instead of RMD, we only store _w_ 0 such that the memory cost is constant w.r.t. the total horizon size _T_ .

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

**Baselines.** We demonstrate the efficacy of our algorithm by comparing to the following baselines. **1) First-Order Approximation (FO).** Computationally the most efficient HO algorithm that completely ignores the second-order term, i.e. _g_[SO] = 0. **2) One-step Look-ahead Approximation (1-step). (Luketina et al., 2016)** The short-horizon approximation where only a single step is unrolled to compute each hypergradient. **3) DrMAD. (Fu et al., 2016)** An approximation of RMD that linearly interpolates between the initial and the last weight to save memory (see Algorithm 2). **4) Neumann IFT (N.IFT). (Lorraine et al., 2020)** An IFT based method that approximates the inverse-Hessian-vector product by Neumann series. Note that this method supports online optimization around convergence. Specifically, among total _T_ = 100 inner-steps, N.IFT( _N, K_ ) means for the last _K_ steps we perform online HO each with _N_ inversion steps. It requires total ( _N_ + 1) _× K_ JVPs. We tune ( _N, K_ ) among _{_ (2 _,_ 25) _,_ (5 _,_ 10) _,_ (10 _,_ 5) _}_ , roughly computing 50 JVPs per inner-opt. **5) HyperDistill.** Our high-dimensional online HO algorithm based on the idea of knowledge distillation. We tune the decaying factor within _γ ∈{_ 0 _._ 9 _,_ 0 _._ 99 _,_ 0 _._ 999 _,_ 0 _._ 9999 _}_ . The linear regression is done every 50 inner-optimization problems.

**Target meta-learning models.** We test on the following three meta-learning models. **1) Almost No Inner Loop (ANIL). (Raghu et al., 2019)** The intuition of ANIL is that the need for task-specific adaptation diminishes when the task distribution is homogeneous. Following this intuition, based on a typical 4-layer convolutional network with 32 channels (Finn et al., 2017), we designate the three bottom layers as the high-dimensional hyperparameter and the 4th convolutional layer and the last fully connected layer as the weight, similarly to Javed & White (2019).

Note that we aim to meta-learn the MLP over a task distribution and apply to diverse unseen tasks, instead of solving a single task. Also, in this meta model the direct gradient is zero, _g_[FO] = 0. In this case, _π[∗]_ in HyperDistill has a meaning of nothing but rescaling the learning rate, so we simply set _π[∗]_ = 1.

**Use of Reptile.** Note that for all the above meta-learning models, we meta-learn the weight initialization with Reptile (Nichol et al., 2018) as well, representing a more practical meta-learning scenario than learning from random initialization. To generate each task, we randomly sample 10 classes from each dataset (5000 examples) and randomly split them into 2500 training and 2500 test examples. **1) TinyImageNet.** (Le & Yang, 2015) This dataset contains 200 classes of general categories. We split them into 100, 40, and 60 classes for meta-training, meta-validation, and meta-test. Each class has 500 examples of size 64 _×_ 64. **2) CIFAR100.** (Krizhevsky et al., 2009) This dataset contains 100 classes of general categories. We split them into 50, 20, and 30 classes for meta-training, meta-validation, and meta-test. Each class has 500 examples of size 32 _×_ 32.

**Experimental setup. Meta-training:** For **inner-optimization** of the weights, we use SGD with momentum 0 _._ 9 and set the learning rate _µ_[Inner] = 0 _._ 1 for MetaWeightNet and _µ_[Inner] = 0 _._ 01 for the others. The number of inner-steps is _T_ = 100 and batchsize is 100. We use random cropping and horizontal flipping as data augmentations. For the **hyperparameter optimization** , we also use SGD with momentum 0 _._ 9 with learning rate _µ_[Hyper] = 0 _._ 01 for MetaWeightNet and _µ_[Hyper] = 0 _._ 001 for the others, which we linearly decay toward 0 over total _M_ = 1000 inner-optimizations. We perform parallel meta-learning with meta-batchsize set to 4. **Meta-testing:** We solve 500 tasks to measure average performance, with exactly the same inner-optimization setup as meta-training. We repeat this over 5 different meta-training runs and report mean and 95% confidence intervals (see Table 2). **Code is publicly available at:** https://github.com/haebeom-lee/hyperdistill

## 5.1 ANALYSIS

We perform the following analysis together with the WarpGrad model and CIFAR100 dataset. **HyperDistill provides faster convergence and better generalization.** Figure 2 shows that HyperDistill shows much faster meta-training convergence than the baselines for all the meta-learning

We see that the convergence of offline method such as DrMAD is significantly worse than a simple first-order method, demonstrating the importance of frequent update via online optimization. HyperDistill shows significantly better convergence than FO and 1-step because it is online and at the same time alleviates the short horizon bias. As a result, Table 2 shows that the meta-test performance of HyperDistill is significantly better than the baselines, although it requires comparable number of JVPs per each inner-optimiztion.

**HyperDistill is a reasonable approximation of the true hypergradient.** We see from Figure 3(a) that the hypergradient obtained from HyperDistill is more similar to the exact RMD than those obtained from FO and 1-step, demonstrating that HyperDistill can actually alleviate the short horizon bias. HyperDistill is even comparable to N.IFT(10 _,_ 1) that computes 11 JVPs, whereas HyperDistill computes only a single JVP. Such results indicate that the approximation we used in Eq. (7) and DrMAD in Eq. (16) are accurate enough. Figure 3(b) shows that with careful tuning of _γ_ (e.g. 0 _._ 99), the direction of the approximated second-order term in Eq. (7) can be much more accurate than the second-order term of 1-step ( _γ_ = 0). In Figure 3(c), as HyperDistill distills such a good approximation, it can provide a better direction of the second-order term than 1-step. Although the gap may seem marginal, even N.IFT(10 _,_ 1) performs similarly, showing that matching the direction of the second-order term without unrolling the full gradient steps is inherently a challenging problem. Figure 4(a) and 4(b) show that the samples collected according to Algorithm 4 is largely linear, supporting our choice of Eq. (15). Figure 4(c) and 4(d) show that the range of fitted _θ_ is accurate and stable, explaining why we do not have to perform the estimation frequently. Note that DrMAD approximation (Eq. (16)) is accurate (Figure 3(a) and 3(c)), helping to predict the hypergradient size. **HyperDistill is compuatationally efficient.** Figure 4(e) shows the superior computational efficiency of HyperDistill in terms of the trade-off between meta-test performance and the amount of JVP computations. Note that wall-clock time is roughly proportional to the number of JVPs per inner-optimization. In Appendix F, we can see that the actual increase in memory cost and wallcock time is very marginal compared to 1-step approximation.

## 6 CONCLUSION

In this work, we proposed a novel HO method, _HyperDistill_ , that can optimize high-dimensional hyperparameters in an online manner. It was done by approximating the exact second-order term with knowledge distillation. We demonstrated that HyperDistill provides faster meta-convergence and better generalization performance based on realistic meta-learning methods and datasets. We also verified that it is thanks to the accurate approximations we proposed.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper introduces HyperDistill, a method for online hyperparameter optimization that uses knowledge distillation to approximate the second-order terms of the hypergradient. By distilling the trajectory information into a single Jacobian-vector product (JVP), the authors aim to achieve the scalability of first-order methods while mitigating short-horizon bias.

The paper addresses a significant challenge in gradient-based meta-learning by proposing a novel, computationally efficient approximation for online hypergradient computation. The empirical results demonstrate strong performance across standard benchmarks, and the proposed distillation technique is both clever and well-motivated by existing limitations in the literature.

Overview issues:

- Unverified Lipschitz Continuity Assumption: In Section 4.2, the derivation of the lower bound for the hypergradient objective (Eq. 10) relies on the Lipschitz continuity of the JVP function $f_t(w, D)$. However, the authors do not provide any evidence or discussion regarding whether this assumption holds for the neural network architectures used in their experiments. Given that these architectures include non-linear warp layers and MLPs with potentially complex loss surfaces, the assumption that the JVP is globally Lipschitz with a manageable constant $K$ is strong. The authors should provide a brief empirical check or theoretical justification for this assumption, as the validity of the distilled objective hinges on it.

- Heuristic Justification for Linear Estimation: The estimation of the scaling factor $\pi_t^*$ in Section 4.3 uses a linear estimator $c_\gamma(t; \theta)$ based on the observation that the lower bound in Eq. 10 is 'roughly proportional' to the sum of discounted JVP norms. While Figure 4 shows that the samples collected are somewhat linear, the paper lacks a formal argument for why this linear form is sufficient to capture the dynamics of the second-order term over a long horizon. If the hyperparameter landscape is highly non-convex or non-stationary, a simple linear model might fail to track the true hypergradient size accurately. A sensitivity analysis or a comparison against a non-linear estimator would strengthen the claim that this heuristic is robust.

- Sensitivity to Hyperparameter $\gamma$: The decay factor $\gamma$ plays a central role in balancing the influence of the past trajectory, effectively acting as a memory parameter. The authors note that $\gamma$ must be tuned carefully, but the paper does not provide a systematic analysis of how sensitive the final meta-learning performance is to the choice of $\gamma$. Given the claim that HyperDistill generalizes one-step lookahead (where $\gamma=0$), it is important to understand the stability of the method across different task distributions. Including a plot showing meta-test performance as a function of $\gamma$ would clarify whether the method is robust or if it requires extensive meta-validation tuning.

- Ambiguity in Dataset Distillation Mechanism: Eq. 14 describes the distillation of the dataset $D$ using random subsampling $\mathbb{S}\mathbb{S}(D, p)$. This is a heuristic approach to managing the 'dataset' component of the hypergradient, but it lacks a clear connection to the underlying optimization objective. It is unclear if random subsampling is an effective proxy for the gradient information contained in the full task distribution. The authors should clarify if this approach is intended to be an approximation of a coreset or if it is purely a computational convenience, and ideally, provide some ablation comparing this to other dataset-weighting strategies.

- Lack of empirical sensitivity analysis for the decay factor gamma: The decay factor gamma is presented as a critical parameter that controls the trade-off between short-horizon bias and long-term memory, effectively generalizing the one-step lookahead approach. However, the paper treats gamma as a hyperparameter to be tuned without providing a systematic sensitivity analysis or a performance profile across a range of values. Readers need to know if the method is robust to the choice of gamma or if the performance gains are highly localized to specific values. I suggest adding a plot showing meta-test performance on the CIFAR100 dataset against varying values of gamma (e.g., from 0.0 to 0.9999) to demonstrate the stability of the HyperDistill algorithm.

- Absence of a concrete ablation study on the dataset distillation heuristic: The mechanism for distilling the dataset D via random subsampling (Eq. 14) is a significant heuristic step in the proposed methodology. It is currently unclear whether this random subsampling is an effective proxy for the gradient information or if it introduces noise that could destabilize the hypergradient. To justify this design choice, the paper should include an ablation study comparing the current random subsampling approach against a baseline that uses a fixed dataset subset or a more structured coreset selection method. This comparison is necessary to determine if the performance improvements are due to the distillation of the JVP or if the dataset subsampling strategy is a primary driver of the results.

- Need for a formal convergence analysis or empirical stability check: While the paper claims HyperDistill is suitable for online optimization, it lacks a discussion or empirical evidence regarding the convergence behavior of the nested optimization loop. The interaction between the online hyperparameter updates and the inner-loop weight updates could lead to instability, especially in non-stationary task distributions. A simple empirical trace of the hypergradient norm over the course of meta-training for one of the benchmark models would provide necessary evidence that the approximation does not diverge. I recommend adding a plot showing the evolution of the hypergradient norm and validation loss over the meta-training steps to verify the stability of the online update process.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Include a sensitivity analysis for the hyperparameter $\gamma$ to demonstrate the robustness of the method across different task distributions.

- Clarify the theoretical or empirical basis for the Lipschitz assumption on the JVP function $f_t(w, D)$ in Section 4.2.

- Add an ablation study for the dataset distillation mechanism (Eq. 14) to justify the use of random subsampling compared to alternative weighting schemes.

- Provide a brief discussion or empirical check on the limitations of the linear estimator $c_\gamma(t; \theta)$ in highly non-stationary environments.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes HyperDistill, a method for online hyperparameter optimization in meta-learning that approximates the second-order hypergradient term using knowledge distillation into a single Jacobian-vector product. The approach aims to balance scalability, online update capability, and reduced short-horizon bias. The experimental results show competitive performance on standard meta-learning benchmarks. However, the theoretical derivation relies on strong assumptions that may not hold in practice, and the empirical gains over simpler baselines are marginal relative to the added complexity. Several methodological choices lack justification, and the comparison with recent literature is incomplete.

The paper addresses a practical bottleneck in meta-learning by attempting to distill long-horizon second-order information into an online-friendly update rule. The idea of approximating the hypergradient via a distilled mapping is interesting and aligns with recent trends in efficient meta-optimization. The experimental setup covers multiple meta-learning architectures and datasets, providing a reasonable empirical baseline. The main weakness lies in the theoretical grounding of the distillation objective and the justification for key approximations. The paper would benefit from stronger analysis of approximation errors, stability under non-stationary dynamics, and a more thorough comparison with modern baselines.

Overview issues:

- Weak theoretical justification for the distillation objective and Lipschitz assumption: The derivation in Section 4.1 minimizes a distillation loss (Eq 4) but immediately replaces it with a lower bound derived from a Lipschitz continuity assumption on the normalized JVP (Eq 9). Deep meta-learning landscapes are highly non-convex, and Jacobians frequently exhibit ill-conditioning or rapid directional changes that violate this assumption. The resulting weighted average of past weights (Eq 12) assumes the optimal distilled point lies on a geodesic between previous steps, which lacks general theoretical support. Maximizing this lower bound does not guarantee minimization of the original distillation error or improved hypergradient accuracy. The authors should provide a theoretical bound on the approximation error introduced by Eq 12 or replace the derivation with an empirical ablation comparing the weighted average to direct optimization of the distillation loss.

- Over-simplified curvature approximation in Eq 7: Section 4.2 replaces the product of Jacobians with a scalar geometric decay. This ignores both the directional curvature and the varying magnitude of true Jacobians across the inner loop. While Figure 3 reports high cosine similarity, this metric only measures directional alignment and says nothing about magnitude accuracy, which is critical for step-size selection in optimization. The scalar decay likely introduces significant bias when the inner loop traverses regions of varying curvature or when the learning rate is large. The authors should analyze the error introduced by this approximation and consider a diagonal or block-diagonal approximation to capture anisotropic curvature without resorting to a single scalar factor.

- Lack of analysis on target drift and online stability: The method distills a target that changes at every meta-step because the hyperparameters are updated online. The distilled weights, datasets, and the linear estimator are updated sequentially, but the paper does not address how the distillation model copes with this rapid target drift. Distilling a moving target can lead to oscillations or catastrophic forgetting of useful curvature information, especially when the inner-loop trajectory shifts significantly between meta-updates. The authors should include an empirical study tracking the drift of the distillation target and the estimator over meta-training steps. Adding a mechanism to regularize the distilled weights or use exponential moving averages would strengthen the online stability claims.

- Questionable computational overhead and marginal empirical gains: The paper claims comparable JVP counts to the 1-step baseline, but the implementation involves computing weighted averages, subsampling datasets, and running a backward pass for linear estimation every 50 steps. The wall-clock and memory overhead are not rigorously measured, and the claim of constant memory contradicts the storage requirements for the backward pass in Algorithm 4. Moreover, the performance gains over 1-step are modest and sometimes inconsistent across datasets. The added complexity may not justify the marginal improvement. The authors should provide a detailed breakdown of wall-clock time and peak memory usage per inner step. An ablation study isolating the contribution of the distillation component versus the linear estimator would clarify the source of the gains.

- Heuristic dataset subsampling strategy lacks justification: Section 4.2 interprets the weighting factors as probabilities for proportional dataset subsampling. This is a heuristic with no theoretical backing. Subsampling changes the data distribution seen by the inner loop, which can alter the trajectory and invalidate the distilled JVP. The paper acknowledges this limitation but does not provide empirical validation that this subsampling strategy actually preserves the hypergradient direction better than using the full batch or other sampling schemes. The authors should justify the subsampling strategy empirically by comparing it against alternatives like stratified sampling or a fixed replay buffer. Reporting how different sampling rates affect cosine similarity and final performance would address this gap.

- Outdated literature positioning and missing baselines: The introduction claims that short-horizon approximation is the only existing method that fully supports online optimization, which overlooks recent advances in online meta-optimization, Hessian-free methods, and other scalable second-order approximations. The baseline comparison also lacks modern online HO methods that have emerged since the paper's submission. This weakens the novelty claim and makes it difficult to assess the true contribution. The authors should update the related work and introduction to acknowledge recent online HO methods. Including comparisons with at least two recent scalable second-order or truncated unrolled methods would contextualize the performance gains and clarify the specific niche HyperDistill occupies.

- Missing worked parametric example for hypergradient approximation: The paper evaluates HyperDistill exclusively on large-scale meta-learning tasks like ANIL, WarpGrad, and MetaWeightNet. Readers cannot easily calibrate the approximation error or understand the behavior of the distillation objective without a simple, fully-specified model. The authors should include a worked example using a standard parametric model, such as linear regression or a 2-layer neural network with a single learning rate hyperparameter. In this setting, the exact hypergradient can be computed analytically or via exact unrolling. Computing Equation (7) and the distilled JVP for this model would let readers verify how the scalar decay gamma affects the direction and magnitude of the second-order term. This would also clarify whether the approximation holds when the loss landscape is ill-conditioned or when the inner loop traverses sharp minima.

- Lack of practical guidance for tuning distillation hyperparameters: The method relies on a geometric decay factor gamma and a periodic linear estimation schedule, yet the paper provides no systematic analysis of how to choose these in practice. The abstract and introduction claim the method is scalable and practical for online optimization, but without guidance on gamma selection or estimation frequency, practitioners may struggle to reproduce the results. The authors should add an ablation study varying gamma across a wider range and testing different estimation periods, such as every 10, 20, 50, and 100 steps. Including a simple heuristic or rule of thumb for setting these based on horizon length T or the inner learning rate would make the method immediately usable. Reporting the sensitivity of the final test accuracy to these choices is essential to validate the online optimization claims.

- Missing verification of approximation recovery and baseline comparisons: The paper claims HyperDistill generalizes short-horizon approximations and avoids their bias, but it never formally verifies that the method recovers known results as a special case. For instance, does the distilled JVP converge to the exact RMD hypergradient as gamma approaches 1 or with increased distillation capacity? The authors should compute the approximation error bound for a simple inner optimizer, like SGD with a constant learning rate, and show how the error scales with gamma and horizon length T. Additionally, the baseline comparison omits recent scalable second-order or truncated unrolled methods that have emerged since the initial submission. Including at least one modern online HO baseline would clarify whether the gains come from the distillation mechanism or simply from better hyperparameter tuning.

- Missing computational analysis for high-dimensional hyperparameter scaling: The scalability claims require a concrete computational analysis breaking down the FLOPs and peak memory usage as a function of hyperparameter dimension and horizon length. The implementation details do not account for the memory required to store the distilled weights, datasets, and the backward pass trajectory for linear estimation. Specifically, the authors need to clarify how the dataset subsampling in Equation (14) affects memory when the training batches are large. A table comparing wall-clock time and memory against the 1-step and DrMAD baselines would substantiate the scalability claims. Without this breakdown, the assertion that the method is truly memory-efficient remains unverified.

Recommendation: Major revision. The core idea of distilling second-order information is promising, but the theoretical derivation relies on assumptions that are rarely satisfied in deep meta-learning, and the empirical gains over simpler baselines are marginal relative to the added complexity. The paper requires stronger analysis of approximation errors, online stability under target drift, and a more thorough comparison with modern baselines before it can be considered for publication.

Revision targets:

- Provide a theoretical or empirical justification for the scalar curvature approximation in Eq 7 and analyze its impact on hypergradient magnitude and optimization stability.

- Include an empirical study tracking target drift and estimator stability over meta-training steps, and propose a smoothing or regularization mechanism to mitigate non-stationary dynamics.

- Add a detailed computational analysis reporting wall-clock time and peak memory usage per inner step, along with an ablation study isolating the distillation and linear estimation components.

- Justify the dataset subsampling strategy in Eq 14 by comparing it against full-batch or alternative sampling methods, and report the impact on hypergradient direction accuracy.

- Update the literature review and baseline comparisons to include recent online hyperparameter optimization methods, clarifying the specific advantages and limitations of HyperDistill.

comments.title: Contradiction between FMD memory complexity in text and Table 1
comments.quote: However, a critical limitation is that the time and space complexity linearly increases w.r.t. the hyperparameter dimension.
comments.explanation: The introduction explicitly states that FMD's space complexity scales linearly with the hyperparameter dimension. This directly contradicts Table 1, which marks FMD as having 'Constant memory' (O). Forward-mode differentiation requires storing Jacobians or intermediate derivatives that grow with the dimension of the differentiated variables, making constant memory infeasible. Please correct Table 1 to mark FMD's constant memory capability as 'X' to align with the textual description.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Maclaurin et al. (2015) memory-saving mechanism
comments.quote: *Maclaurin et al. (2015)* proposes a more memory-efficient RMD, which reverses the SGD trajectory with momentum.
comments.explanation: This claim incorrectly attributes the memory efficiency of Maclaurin et al. (2015) to momentum. That paper introduces reversible learning (using reversible neural network blocks or activation checkpointing) to avoid storing intermediate forward-pass activations, thereby reducing memory for RMD. Momentum is an optimizer update rule and has no role in reversing trajectories or reducing memory in this context. Rewrite the sentence to accurately describe the reversible learning or checkpointing contribution.

comments.title: Unused interpolated weight in Algorithm 2 pseudocode
comments.quote:
|  6: α ← αAt | 6: gSO ← gSO + αBt  |
|  7: end for | 7: α ← αAt  |
comments.explanation: The pseudocode computes $ŷ_{t-1}$ in line 5 but never passes it to the Jacobian evaluations in lines 6 and 7. As written, Algorithm 2 performs the exact same trajectory-dependent computation as Algorithm 1, failing to implement the claimed DrMAD approximation. Add a directive after line 5 specifying that $A_t$ and $B_t$ must be evaluated at the interpolated weight $ŷ_{t-1}$, e.g., 'evaluate $A_t, B_t$ at $ŷ_{t-1}$', to correctly approximate the hypergradient along the straight-line trajectory.

comments.title: Unexplained 2t-1 JVP coefficient in DrMAD complexity
comments.quote: For each online HO step  $t = 1, \dots, T$  we need to compute  $2t - 1$  JVPs, thus the number of total JVPs to complete a single trajectory accumulates up to  $\sum_{t=1}^{T}(2t - 1) = T^{2}$
comments.explanation: The derivation of $2t-1$ JVPs per step is not explained. Standard reverse-mode unrolling computes all hypergradients in a single backward pass using $T$ JVPs total. If the authors are computing hypergradients incrementally at each step $t$, the cost would be $\\sum_{t=1}^T t = T(T+1)/2$, not $T^2$. The factor of 2 suggests either forward and backward passes are both computed, or both $A$ and $B$ terms require separate JVP evaluations per step. Please clarify the algorithmic steps that lead to $2t-1$ and justify why a single reverse pass cannot achieve $O(T)$ JVPs.

comments.title: Memory complexity claim contradicts recursive algorithm requirements
comments.quote: l JVPs. Also, as we use DrMAD instead of RMD, we only store  $w_{0}$  such that the memory cost is constant w.r.t. the total horizon size  $T$ .
comments.explanation: The recursive definitions in Eqs. (17) and (18) explicitly require access to intermediate trajectory states $w_{T-s}$ and $D_{T-s+1}$ for $s=2,\\dots,T$. Computing these from $w_0$ alone would require re-running the inner optimization loop, increasing computational cost to $O(T^2)$ per meta-step, which contradicts the claim of marginal cost. Therefore, the algorithm must store $O(T)$ checkpoints (or use a checkpointing scheme), making the memory complexity $O(T)$, not constant. Rewrite the memory claim to specify that $O(T)$ trajectory checkpoints are stored, because the recursive definition in Eq. (17) explicitly requires access to $w_{T-s}$ for $s=2,\\dots,T$, which cannot be retrieved from $w_0$ alone without additional storage or recomputation.

comments.title: Contradiction between 1-step claim and JVP count
comments.quote: 2) One-step Look-ahead Approximation (1-step). (Luketina et al., 2016) The short-horizon approximation where only a single step is unrolled to compute each hypergradient.
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states that this baseline unrolls only a single step, which typically requires one Jacobian-vector product per hyperparameter dimension or a small constant number. Table 2, however, reports 50 JVPs per inner optimization for this method. This discrepancy is significant and obscures the actual computational cost of the baseline. It is unclear whether the 50 JVPs stem from processing a batch of 50 samples, unrolling more steps than described, or a different implementation detail. Rewrite the baseline description to clarify the exact computation that yields 50 JVPs, or adjust the reported JVP count in Table 2 to match the single-step unrolling claim.

comments.title: Contradiction between analysis scope and generalization claim
comments.quote: We perform the following analysis together with the WarpGrad model and CIFAR100 dataset.
comments.explanation: The opening sentence explicitly restricts the analysis to a single model-dataset pair. The very next paragraph, however, claims that Figure 2 demonstrates faster convergence 'for all the meta-learning models and datasets we considered.' This is a direct internal contradiction. The authors must either clarify that the analysis was actually run across all datasets and models, or temper the claim to apply only to the WarpGrad/CIFAR100 setting to match the stated scope.

comments.title: Causal link between approximation accuracy and performance is overstated
comments.quote: We also verified that it is thanks to the accurate approximations we proposed.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims a verified causal link between the method's performance gains and the accuracy of the proposed approximations. However, the paper's evaluation relies primarily on cosine similarity to measure approximation quality, which only captures directional alignment and ignores magnitude accuracy. Since hypergradient-based optimization depends critically on both direction and scale, directional similarity alone cannot verify that approximation accuracy drives the observed performance improvements. The authors should either conduct ablation studies that isolate the distillation component's effect on final performance or rephrase this statement to reflect that the method achieves better results while maintaining high directional alignment. Remove the causal claim because the empirical evidence provided does not establish a direct link between approximation accuracy and performance gains.

comments.title: Notational collision in proxy objective definition
comments.quote:
we have

$\hat{\pi}_{t}(w,D)\approx\hat{\pi}_{t}(w,D)=\sum_{i=1}^{t}\delta_{t,i}\cdot f_{t}(w,D)^{\text{T}}f_{t}(w_{i-1},D_{i})$ (8)
comments.explanation: The equation uses the same symbol $\\hat{\\pi}_{t}(w,D)$ for both the original objective and the derived proxy, which directly contradicts the subsequent sentence: 'Instead of maximizing $\\hat{\\pi}_{t}$ directly, we now maximize $\\hat{\\pi}_{t}$ w.r.t. $w$ and $D$ as a proxy objective.' This notational collision obscures the distinction between the true second-order term and the distillation proxy. Rewrite the equation as $\\hat{\\pi}_{t}(w,D)\\approx\\tilde{\\pi}_{t}(w,D)=\\sum_{i=1}^{t}\\delta_{t,i}\\cdot f_{t}(w,D)^{\\text{T}}f_{t}(w_{i-1},D_{i})$ and update the following sentence to reference $\\tilde{\\pi}_{t}$ to maintain logical consistency.

comments.title: Inaccurate JVP count description for Neumann IFT
comments.quote: . We tune  $(N, K)$  among  $\{(2, 25), (5, 10), (10, 5)\}$ , roughly computing 50 JVPs per inner-op
comments.explanation: The paper provides a formula stating that Neumann IFT requires $(N + 1) \\times K$ JVPs. Applying this formula to the tuned pairs yields 75, 60, and 55 JVPs respectively. Describing these values as 'roughly 50' is mathematically inaccurate, as the actual counts are 10% to 50% higher than stated. The table correctly lists the set {55, 60, 75}, but the accompanying text misrepresents the magnitude. Rewrite 'roughly computing 50 JVPs per inner-opt.' to 'computing 55, 60, or 75 JVPs per inner-opt. depending on the tuned (N, K) pair' to align the text with the provided formula and table values.

comments.title: Missing metric for performance comparison
comments.quote: HyperDistill is even comparable to N.IFT(10, 1) that computes 11 JVPs, whereas HyperDistill computes only a single JVP.
comments.explanation: The statement that HyperDistill is 'comparable to N.IFT(10, 1)' leaves the comparison metric undefined. The surrounding text discusses Figure 3(a) and cosine similarity to the exact RMD hypergradient, so the authors likely mean the approximation quality is similar. Without explicitly naming the metric, readers cannot verify the claim. Rewrite the sentence to specify the metric, for example: 'HyperDistill achieves a cosine similarity comparable to N.IFT(10, 1)...'

comments.title: Unjustified assumption linking JVP count to wall-clock time
comments.quote: Note that wall-clock time is roughly proportional to the number of JVPs per inner-optimization.
comments.explanation: Treating wall-clock time as directly proportional to JVP count oversimplifies the computational reality of deep learning training. Runtime is heavily influenced by memory allocation, autograd graph overhead, and data pipeline bottlenecks, which do not scale linearly with JVP operations. This assumption weakens the efficiency argument presented in Figure 4(e). The authors should either report actual wall-clock measurements to validate the proportionality or rephrase the claim to state that JVP count serves as a proxy for computational cost rather than a direct measure of runtime.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes HyperDistill, a novel hyperparameter optimization method that uses knowledge distillation to approximate second-order terms, enabling online optimization with scalability to high-dimensional hyperparameters and long horizons. The empirical results show promising improvements over baselines on meta-learning benchmarks. However, several methodological concerns and missing analyses weaken the paper's claims.

Overview issues:

- Convergence of the distillation approximation is not guaranteed: The paper approximates the second-order term by distilling it into a single JVP, but provides no theoretical guarantee that the distilled JVP converges to the true hypergradient, especially under non-convex objectives common in deep learning. The Lipschitz continuity assumption on the Hessian (Eq. 9) is critical for deriving the lower bound in Eq. 10, yet this assumption is rarely verified in practice and may be violated for neural networks with non-smooth activations or complex loss landscapes. The paper should discuss the implications of this assumption and ideally provide empirical checks (e.g., monitoring the Lipschitz constant during training) or alternative relaxations.

- Scalability claims lack rigorous empirical validation: The paper claims scalability to high-dimensional hyperparameters and long horizons, but the experiments only consider hyperparameter dimensions up to thousands (e.g., ANIL with 3 convolutional layers) and horizons of 100 steps. No experiments demonstrate performance on hyperparameters with >10^4 dimensions or horizons >100 steps, which are common in modern meta-learning (e.g., full-network fine-tuning). The paper should include such experiments or explicitly state the limitations of the method for extreme scales. Additionally, the number of JVPs per inner-opt for HyperDistill is reported as ≈58, which is not significantly lower than Neumann IFT (55-75 JVPs), weakening the scalability advantage.

- Sensitivity to the choice of γ and distillation schedule is not analyzed: The decaying factor γ is a critical hyperparameter that controls how much past trajectory is considered (γ=0 reduces to 1-step, γ=1 considers all steps). The paper tunes γ over {0.9, 0.99, 0.999, 0.9999} but does not analyze how sensitive the method is to this choice across different tasks or datasets. Similarly, the distillation schedule (EstimationPeriod set to every 50 inner-optimizations) is chosen without justification or ablation. The paper should include a sensitivity analysis for γ and the estimation frequency, showing how performance varies and providing guidance for practitioners.

- Comparison with state-of-the-art HO methods is incomplete: The paper compares against FO, 1-step, DrMAD, and Neumann IFT, but omits several recent scalable HO methods such as HOAG (Pedregosa, 2016), FOL (Shaban et al., 2019), and RMD with checkpointing (Grefenstette et al., 2019). Including these baselines would strengthen the claim that HyperDistill is state-of-the-art. Additionally, the Neumann IFT baseline is used with a limited tuning of (N,K) that may not be optimal; a more thorough hyperparameter search for baselines would ensure fair comparison.

- Computational cost analysis lacks wall-clock measurements: The paper uses the number of JVPs per inner-optimization as a proxy for computational cost, but this ignores overhead from the distillation process (e.g., computing w_t* and D_t*, fitting the linear estimator). Table 2 shows HyperDistill uses ≈58 JVPs, which is comparable to Neumann IFT (55-75 JVPs), but the actual wall-clock time or memory usage is not reported. The paper should include wall-clock time and memory measurements for all methods to substantiate the claim of computational efficiency, as is standard in this field.

- Reproducibility details are insufficient: While the paper states code is available, it does not report random seeds or provide detailed hyperparameter settings for baselines (e.g., learning rates, momentum, decay schedules for FO, 1-step, DrMAD, Neumann IFT). The confidence intervals are based on only 5 meta-training runs, which is low for deep learning experiments. The paper should report seeds, provide a table of all hyperparameters, and ideally increase the number of runs to improve statistical reliability.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add a theoretical analysis or empirical verification of the Lipschitz continuity assumption and the convergence of the distillation approximation.

- Include experiments on higher-dimensional hyperparameters (e.g., >10^4) and longer horizons (e.g., >100 steps) to validate scalability claims.

- Perform a sensitivity analysis for γ and the estimation frequency, and provide guidance on choosing these hyperparameters.

- Add comparisons with additional state-of-the-art HO methods (e.g., HOAG, FOL, RMD with checkpointing) and report wall-clock time and memory usage for all methods.

- Improve reproducibility by reporting random seeds, detailed hyperparameter settings for all baselines, and increasing the number of meta-training runs.

coarse__glm-4.7-flash.label: Coarse (glm-4.7-flash)
coarse__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes HyperDistill, an online hyperparameter optimization (HO) method for meta-learning that aims to approximate the second-order hypergradient using a single JVP via knowledge distillation. The method relies on a weighted average of past weights and a linear regression to estimate the scaling factor. While the empirical results show improvements over baselines, the theoretical foundation is weak, the 'online' claim is inconsistent with the implementation, and the scalability claims are contradicted by the memory requirements.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by attempting to bridge the gap between online optimization and the accuracy of full horizon methods. However, the theoretical derivation of the update rule relies on maximizing a loose lower bound rather than the actual objective, and the computational claims regarding memory usage conflict with the actual implementation requirements. The empirical success is promising but not fully supported by the rigorous theoretical framework presented.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical Justification of the Update Rule: The paper derives the update rule for the distilled weights $w_t^*$ (Eq. 13) by maximizing a lower bound on the alignment score $	ilde{	heta}_t$ (Eq. 10), rather than maximizing the original objective (Eq. 6). While maximizing a lower bound is a valid heuristic, the derivation does not prove that this maximizes the actual alignment $f_t(w_t^*, D_t^*)^	op g_t^{SO}$. The Lipschitz continuity assumption (Eq. 9) is extremely strong for SGD dynamics on deep networks, and if the bound is loose, the resulting $w_t^*$ and $D_t^*$ will be suboptimal. The paper needs to either prove that the bound is tight under the model assumptions or justify the heuristic more rigorously, perhaps by analyzing the approximation error introduced by the Lipschitz bound.

- Misleading 'Online' Characterization: The paper claims to support 'Online optimization' in Table 1 and the abstract, implying updates occur at every inner step. However, Algorithm 3 (HyperDistill) only updates the hyperparameters $	heta$ and $	heta_{	ext{Reptile}}$ every 50 inner-optimization completions (due to the 'EstimationPeriod' setting). This contradicts the strict definition of online optimization used in the introduction (e.g., FMD updates every step) and makes the comparison to the '1-step' baseline unfair, as 1-step updates every step while HyperDistill updates every 50 steps. The paper should clarify whether it targets 'frequent' updates or true online updates and adjust the comparison metrics accordingly.

- Unjustified Linear Estimator Assumption: The scaling factor $	heta$ is estimated using a linear function of the form $c_	heta(t) = 	heta 
orm{v_t}  rac{1-	heta^s}{1-	heta}$ (Eq. 15). The paper justifies this form based on the structure of the lower bound in Eq. 10, but this is an empirical assumption, not a theoretical guarantee. The magnitude of the true hypergradient $g_t^{SO}$ is a sum of products of Jacobians, which does not necessarily scale linearly with the number of steps or the norm of a single JVP $v_t$. If the underlying dynamics change (e.g., curvature changes), this linear relationship could break down. The paper should analyze the sensitivity of the estimator to non-linear dynamics or provide a theoretical bound on the estimation error.

- Scalability vs. Memory Contradiction: Table 1 claims HyperDistill supports 'High-dim.' hyperparameters and 'Constant memory'. However, the update rule for $w_t^*$ (Eq. 13) requires storing the history of weights $w_0, w_1, 	ext{...}, w_{t-1}$ to compute the weighted average. If the hyperparameters are truly high-dimensional (e.g., millions of parameters as suggested in the intro), storing the entire trajectory history is computationally infeasible and contradicts the 'constant memory' claim. The paper should address this contradiction by either proving that the method can be implemented with a sliding window or by acknowledging that the memory cost scales with the horizon length $T$.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a rigorous theoretical justification for why maximizing the lower bound (Eq. 10) yields a good approximation of the true hypergradient alignment, or explicitly state the approximation error.

- Clarify the 'online' nature of the method by either updating at every step or adjusting the comparison to 1-step to reflect the lower update frequency.

- Address the memory scalability contradiction by either proving a constant-memory implementation or acknowledging the $O(T)$ memory cost for high-dimensional hyperparameters.

- Provide an analysis of the linear estimator's robustness to non-linear dynamics, ideally with a theoretical bound on the estimation error of $	heta$.

comments.title: Missing formal proof for online optimization
comments.quote: In this work, we proposed a novel HO method, HyperDistill, that can optimize high-dimensional hyperparameters in an online manner.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts that the method supports online optimization, yet the Formal Results section lacks the necessary theorems or proofs to establish this claim. Without a formal derivation of the online update dynamics, the claim remains unsubstantiated.

comments.title: Scalability claim lacks theoretical justification
comments.quote: Our method allows online optimization and also is scalable to the hyperparameter dimension and the horizon length.
comments.explanation: The Abstract explicitly states the method is scalable to high-dimensional hyperparameters. However, the Formal Results section does not contain a theorem or proof verifying this scalability; it merely cites related work. The authors must provide a theoretical justification for this claim.

comments.title: Experimental claims not present in Formal Results
comments.quote: We demonstrated that HyperDistill provides faster meta-convergence and better generalization performance based on realistic meta-learning methods and datasets.
comments.explanation: The Discussion claims experimental success, but the Formal Results section is a literature review and does not contain the experimental results or the theoretical proofs of convergence. The Discussion relies on results that are not present in the provided text.
